speaker: interviewer
text: --with Mr. Mark Weidman. So Weidman, we left off-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I'll repeat this. Tape three, interview with Mr. Mark Weidman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Weidman, we left off where you got an offer from, I think, your cousin [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with the chance of escaping or moving to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us what happened at that point? You-- sorry. You-- you went to your mother. And your mother was very emotional about it, but she advised you to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother advised me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What' happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, my father advised me. I remember a few hundred marks had to be paid for it. My father dug out the money and gave it to me. And I met the-- there was a half-Polish, half-Ukrainian fellow. His name was Voloshyn. His father was a-- a ranger in the-- in the-- in the woods not far from Drohobycz, in a place called Lishnya. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we made the arrangements. So he had his transport going back to Germany. He had to go back a few days-- a couple of days after we met. And-- but I didn't dare to take the train from Drohobycz, because I was well known. I had a lot of people know me. I didn't dare to board a train in the town. Don't forget, at that time, this was just before the ghetto was created in-- in Drohobycz. As a matter of fact, it was the beginning of the ghetto already. And we had to change our-- our living quarters. We had to go to a place which shared maybe 10-- 10 families in one apartment. It was pretty, pretty bad. And one night, the brother of that Voloshyn whose permit I bought, came to me to pick me up at evening and take me to Lishnya. It was a cold, wintry night. It was probably the middle of November. There was already snow on the ground. It was cold, freezing. And in those days, we had to wear a-- a band on the left arm-- a white band with the-- with the-- with the blue David star-- that we should be recognized as Jews. Of course, I had to-- I had to take off the band. And before I went there, I went to my cousin-- to that cousin, Naftali Backenroth, and-- to say goodbye to him, to thank him for what he did for me. And sure enough, there was another friend of ours, a Mr. [? Liever. ?] He's still alive. He lives in Queens, New York. He said, let me just take a look at your pockets, what you have in your pockets before you leave. Sure enough, he found in the-- in the pocket a family picture of my grandfather with a beard. And he took it away from me. He says, if they find that picture with you or on you, you're-- you're-- you're dead. So I remembered-- I remembered that since this. And we started walking to Lishnya. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took off the band. And I was nervous, because I was so-- so accustomed to it. And I was nervous. And we just passed the boundaries of the city. The Ukrainian police stopped us, because there was a curfew. I believe 10 o'clock at night, you couldn't-- nobody was supposed to leave the-- leave the house. And he stopped us, because it was past 10 o'clock. And he's leading us to so-called gendarmerie, the German-- German Schutzpolizei. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here, I felt, here's the end of my career. I just left house. And-- and I'm going to Lishnya. And they've caught me already. They brought us to the police station. And I looked at this brother of the Voloshyn. He was white like a-- like a sheet. He lost his speech. He couldn't-- he couldn't talk, because he had a lot to lose. I had nothing to lose. He had a lot to lose. He was scared. He was stiff. He couldn't talk. He was speechless. And it-- it-- as it turned out, the-- the-- the policeman, the German policeman who was on duty there, he was from the-- from-- from Silesia. This is the German-Polish border. And he spoke Polish. And I paraded as a-- as a Pollack, as a Polish man. He-- as it turned out, he didn't like Ukrainians himself. And I-- I told him what this story is. I said I just came back from Germany. And I showed him this permit that I have and-- for a so-called Urlaub, for my vacation. And I came here. And it was-- we-- we had a-- we had a couple of coffee drinks in-- in the gas-- in the station, the railway station. It was late. And we're going home. He said, that's fine. He gave me-- as a matter-- he offered me a pack of cigarettes. And he ordered the Ukrainian policeman he should-- he should guard us and bring us back all the way to Lishnya. That policeman, I didn't want to have him. But that police was cursing, what kind of trouble we got him into. [INAUDIBLE] he was in that frost in the freezing weather. [INAUDIBLE] a few kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to him, you know something? Go home. Go home. Get lost. I'm-- I'll-- I'll never report you to the police-- because I didn't want him around me. And we went back to Lishnya.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to Lishnya. And there was a brother-in-law of my cousin Naftali Backenroth and two other cousins-- I mean, a cousin with his wife-- Backenroths-- that were hiding by this guy, Voloshyn, in Lishnya. They were hiding under the boards. They dig-- dug out a hole under the-- under the boards, under the-- of the house. They were hiding there. But they-- they knew we were-- I was coming. They came out. They wanted to find out from me what's going on. But I still managed, before I left, to get a-- a blank form of a birth certificate. A Polish certificate. And the brother-in-law of a doctor-- of my cousin Naftali Backenroth, he was a doctor. He was a medical doctor. Dr. Szmer. He was one-- he was hiding there. And I told him I-- he should fill out my birth certificate, because I had no paper, no documents whatsoever, besides this-- the document I'm going to have. And we filled it out. We decided the name-- what kind of a name to fill out. A name should sound either Polish or Ukrainian. We decided on the name Stefan [? Galuska. ?] He fill it out as-- with his handwriting. That was my only document I had, which was, of course, not sufficient. And the next-- I slept over that night. And the next night, we-- we went to a next village, where was a railroad station. Small village. And that's-- that railroad was heading towards Lwow. [INAUDIBLE] the transport of those workers from-- was going from Lwow to Germany. And I was supposed to go with the train to Lwow at night. Dark at night. It was maybe 1 o'clock in the morning. I can't remember. And this guy, the actual guy that came for-- for that so-called Urlaub, he was-- accompanied me to Lwow. When we came to Lwow, it was daylight already. And there was an Aktion going in-- in process. I saw they were-- they were hurting Jews on the street. And-- and-- but there was a big camp in Lwow called Janowska. And whether they-- they were-- whether they were herding them to Janowska or to extermination camp, I don't know. Anyway, this was my greetings in Lwow. And it felt very uncomfortable. As a matter of fact, I went to a barber shop, because somehow I thought my eyebrows may give me away. I have too thick of eyebrows. So I went to the barber shop to get a haircut and a shave. And I told the barber he should thin out my eyebrows. He looked a little, let's see, funny at me, but he-- he didn't suspect me that I'm a Jew. But he thinned them out. And then I went to the railroad station in-- in-- with Voloshyn, together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there was the gathering of all these workers. And they had to go to a public bath. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before they boarded, you had to get a piece of paper that you participated in the public bath, because the Germans were afraid to [INAUDIBLE] epidemics and so forth. I didn't-- I wasn't anxious to go to the public bath, because I know if I go to that public bath and they see me that I am circumcised, I give myself away right away, because in Europe, only Jews were-- were circumcised. The general population was not circumcised-- not like in America. So this was one-- one thing to-- it could-- could have given me away immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to this fellow Voloshyn, you know, you go to bath with them. I mean, you will be coming back [INAUDIBLE] I'll sneak into your group. I'll take my attache case. And I'll go into the-- to railway station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened. I was staying-- I was staying inside. They went. And about an hour or so later, they came. And I duck in into the-- to that group. And I boarded the train going to Germany. I would like to make a remark why I decided also to take this route. I always remembered my father was-- was telling me when I was a kid that the safest place for a-- for a-- for a thief is to hide in the attic of the police station. The police will be-- be looking for him all over town. They'll never think he's hiding over the attic. To me, Germany was that proverbial attic. And sure enough, they-- they did not suspect me most of the time that I am a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would just like to clarify a few things. When you left Drohobycz, your family was in the ghetto? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family was still in the ghetto. My mother was still alive. My-- my grandmother was killed by then. She was killed in 1942 during an Aktion. And I remember finding her among the pile of dead people, because she was killed in her own bed. They wanted to take her to deport her, my grandmother. She was 93 years of age. And she said-- she said to the SS, if you want to kill me, you kill me in my-- right in my own bed. And they did. They shot her in her own bed. Later, after the Aktion of-- I-- me and my father-- it was in the summer of '42-- were looking for her. And we found her. I recognized her by her underwear. She had striped underwear. But her face was swollen-- I mean, recognizable, but already decomposing. And there were about 50 victims at that time. And we gathered them on the Jewish cemetery. And my-- my father and I and some other Jewish people, we dug a mass grave and we buried them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father and your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were still--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] were still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when-- and you left them there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you went on your way-- They were still in the ghetto. --to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The railroad station where you boarded the train, am I to understand that was in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- to get to Lwow, I boarded in a little village between Lishnya and Sambir. I don't remember the name of the-- of the station. A little railroad station. [INAUDIBLE] railroad station that we-- we boarded the train to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You boarded the train. And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train-- the train was heading to Germany. It was-- I didn't know the exact-- the exact destination of the train. But I knew that it was going to stop in Leipzig. That I knew. But as you were-- I found myself in that train, there was a pretty bad experience. It was mostly Ukrainians, some Polish people. They-- they were volunteers working in-- in jobs in Germany. And their main subject was-- was-- indeed, they spoke about it with a gusto-- the destruction of the Jewish population. As a matter of fact, as the train was passing towns-- and so in certain towns I saw in broad daylight-- I saw Germans shooting-- shooting Jewish victims. We-- and they were falling. Standing with rifles, shooting them, and falling down. And the-- the-- my co-passengers, they were laughing and enjoying-- enjoying the view. And I, in order not to be suspect, I had to laugh along. It was a very sad, sad experience that-- that I was going through then. But in order to survive, I had to play that role. I had no other way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then, when you arrived in Leipzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived in Leipzig, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- first of all, let me go back for a moment. I-- I promised Voloshyn once I come to Germany, they'll mail it back to him the permit, the border permit, because actually, the border permit was useless to me. And he wanted to-- to use it again and go back. So I did. That's the first thing I did. I promised him I'll do it, and I did it. I didn't want to leave a bad name after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I found myself with two small valises in my hands and in Leipzig. I didn't know where to turn, where to go, what to say. And I remember the gendarmerie was patrolling the railroad stations. They had such metal-- metal-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like, what do they call it? A-- A breastplate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Breastplates. Yeah. And they were having these-- these-- these high Schutzpolizei caps. They-- they were different in-- than they wore in Poland. And I was avoiding them, of course. I didn't want them they should stop me. And I went on the street. And lo and behold, I-- I saw a guy, civilian guy, who-- who wore on the lapel-- I believe the color of the lapel was yellow. The-- the color of the square was yellow, with a purple P on it, because foreigners, especially from Eastern Europe, they had to wear something recognizable they're not Germans, because they were also restricted in their movements. They were restricted in the places they can go to and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I assumed he must be a Polish guy. I approached him. And I said to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look, I'm stranded here. I am-- I'm a Polish man. And the reason I'm stranded here was I was working for a-- for a-- for a German farmer-- German farmer, and he mistreated me. And I ran away from him. And I would like to have a place where to work and [INAUDIBLE] myself. He said, come-- come along with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in the so-called Gemeinschaftshaus. Gemeinschaftshaus was like a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building where-- where there-- all these foreign workers were living there. Living quarters for the foreign workers. And he found me. He gave me a place. They were like bunks-- three-story bunks. He said, you know, I live in this bunk. But two stories above me, a bunk is vacant. Use that bunk. So I had already a place where to be. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But this was not the-- my final solution yet. I had to do something. I had to either work or-- because-- because I had to eat. I had to dress myself, to live. And this was not a-- it was just a temporary quarters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what I did is [INAUDIBLE]. I would there was one Italian guy living in that-- in that Gemeinschaftshaus. And he-- he was a privileged guy. The Italians and Hungarians, they were the so-called allies of Germany. And they had privileges. They could go-- they could go to movies. They could go to different restaurants, different places, and so forth. So I figured, being that I am already-- I'm already illegal anyhow, let me go for the-- let me go for the best I-- I can get. And I-- and I asked this Italian guy, look, you know, I'm a Polish man. And, you know, I have no privileges or nothing. I didn't tell him all my background, where-- where I came from. I would like to buy your Kennkarte.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This would give me certain-- see, I would affix my-- my picture to it. And I will leave. I will leave town and leave from here. And I will go. And you-- you can-- two weeks later, you can-- you can-- you can write to your hometown in Italy. They'll give you a duplicate. You'll have no problem. You lost it. He liked the idea. And I had a-- a few hundred marks my father gave me. And I sewed it into my sleeve, in my-- in-- in top of my sleeve in my-- of my coat. So I gave it to him. And here I had already a Kennkarte as an Italian. I took-- I had-- I took a picture of mine. I had a little picture of mine. I replaced it. And as-- as bad-- as good or as badly as I made it, I [? peeled ?] the-- the corner of the seal and I pasted it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I have already a Kennkarte. I'm Italian. I don't speak Italian. But I have something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I boarded a train and left this place. I boarded a train. And I was-- the train was heading towards-- go west. I want to go as far west as possible from my hometown. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was in one town or the other town. Was-- I was-- it'd be too much to tell you how much-- all-- all my adventures in all these places. Finally, I will tell you where I wound up. I wound up finally in Gelsenkirchen. Gelsenkirchen is a town in Westphalia. After all my adventures, which were not very successful, I decided to go to the Arbeitsamt in Gelsenkirchen. And I came there. Of course, I-- I played a role that I don't speak German, although I spoke a perfect German. There was always a translator for me. I came there with a translator. I told the translator I came from Poland. I was on the transport of volunteers to-- to Germany. [INAUDIBLE] a railroad station. And I went-- in the station, I-- I left the train to have a-- have a drink of water. And the train left me. And I was left without anything, without-- even without documents. All I have is-- is that birth certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, it's fine. We need people in the coal mines here. We'll have you work in the coal mines. And I figured now I am saved. I am already-- finally I'll have a place where I can be legal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before you do that, you have to go for a-- for a medical exam. And I went to a-- so he gave me the address where I go for medical exam. And I come to that place. And there was a big sign with a big eagle with a swastika. NSDAP, which is Nazi party-- Nationalsozialistische Deutsche Arbeiterpartei-- was the acronym. And there was already a-- a translator. There was-- he always had a translator for these foreign workers. And he examined me. I had to strip naked. He noticed I'm circumcised. He noticed that something is-- but he asked the translator he should ask me whether I had some operation when I was a kid on my genitals. I-- so I said, yes. As far as I remember, my mother told me-- she said I had some infection. And that worked. No suspicion that I am a Jew at all. And it worked. God was with me, apparently, [LAUGHS] watching over me. And I was-- I was allotted a place in the Gemeinschaftshaus on [? Scharnhorststrasse. ?] I remember either 13 or 19. I don't remember the exact number there. We were all foreign workers. And I was assigned to a coal mine in-- in Wattenscheid. Watten was near Gelsenkirchen. Zeche Holland. Zeche means [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coal mine. The name of the coal mine was Holland. It has nothing to do with Holland as a country. But they call it, the coal mine, Zeche Holland. And I was assigned there. And I found-- found myself about 1,500 feet below in the coal mine. It was really awful. Hot. The air was-- was full of dust-- of coal dust. When I was coming out from the coal mine and I looked in the mirror, all I saw is my-- the whites of my eyes. Totally black. The type of work we had to-- were to-- we had to do there-- they were blasting the coal mines. We were blasting coal. And we had to load the blasted coal into little wagons that were rolling along. Those-- those wagons had to be always full. 10 hours a day. It was slavery. [INAUDIBLE] at that time, I did not smoke. And we used to get every month or every two-- two weeks-- I don't remember-- a-- a ration of cigarettes. So I befriended the so-called Obersteiger. He was the foreman in the-- in the mine. And he was also-- he was speaking Polish. He was speaking German. He was from Silesia, which there were coal mines in-- their-- their experience in coal mines [INAUDIBLE] brought him here, probably. And I was keeping giving him those-- bribing him with my cigarettes. He liked me so much that after a while, he tells me, Stefan, I'll give an easier job than this here. Apparently bribery worked. And he brought me into a room. It's called [GERMAN]. A [GERMAN] is a-- a [GERMAN] is a room where-- where-- it was the whole brain of the factory-- of the coal mine. Buttons and switches. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was instructing me, because the guy that was-- that was supposed to be there was-- took sick or something. I don't remember what it was. And I should work there. For about a couple hours, he was showing me all the lights, and the switches, and so forth. And he was watching me whether I do the-- the proper things. And I-- I was doing it. But really, my mind was elsewhere. I couldn't concentrate. Those days, I could not concentrate. I was-- my mind was always in my family back home and with my situation. And after a few hours, finally I-- I heard a-- a big crash. And I didn't know what happened. Apparently I pushed-- pulled the wrong switch. And the-- and the-- the elevators that were taking down the-- the workers were knocked out. This Obersteiger, this so-called friend of mine, he came down. He's beating the-- the daylight out of me, what I caused. The whole-- the whole coal mine was-- was-- was stranded in that coal mine for a few hours-- it'd be for 10 hours. But once they-- they-- they fixed it, the SA came down. They grabbed me-- not only me, all my friends, whoever was friendly with me-- and they arrested us. And they brought us to the Polizeipraesidium, which is the-- which is the Gestapo, the name of the Gestapo, in the main city of Bochum. And they brought us to Bochum. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I cannot-- it's hard to describe how tortured I was in that cell. I mean, in that-- in that prison. Investigations. Constant investigations day and night. They-- they-- sometimes they-- they gave me a big bowl of ground-- something ground. It looked like meat. And then for three days, no food at all. And they were really torturing me. One day-- and they were always investigating. One day-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was always, of course, a translator-- I was sitting in the room. And they were investigating me. And there was one dark-haired and one redhead Gestapo man. And I-- I heard the dark-haired guy say to the redhead man-- say, you know, I think this guy is harmless. Maybe we should let him go.
